1: The Song of Songs (shir hashirim) is the OT's wisdom-meditation on human love and sexuality. Its literal level — a celebration of erotic love between a man and a woman — is taken seriously by responsible interpreters as a canonical affirmation of marriage and the goodness of sexual love within covenant. The allegorical level — YHWH's love for Israel (the Jewish interpretation) or Christ's love for the church (the dominant Christian interpretation) — has been the dominant hermeneutical approach through most of church history (Origen's commentary and Bernard of Clairvaux's 86 sermons on Song 1-2 are the most extensive examples). The allegorical reading is supported by the OT's consistent use of the husband-wife metaphor for YHWH-Israel (Isa 54:5; Jer 2:2; Ezek 16; Hos 1-3) and the NT's application of the bride-bridegroom image to Christ-church (Eph 5:25-32; Rev 19:7-9; 21:2). The two readings are not mutually exclusive: the literal is the foundation that gives the allegorical its force.
2: יִשָּׁקֵנִי (yishaqeni) — 'let him kiss me' is a jussive expressing longing desire, not a statement of fact. The Hebrew nashaq covers not only romantic kissing but the greeting kiss of family reunion and the covenant kiss (David and Jonathan, 1 Sam 20:41). דֹּדֶיךָ (dodekha) — 'your loves/your love' uses the same root dod that names the beloved throughout the Song. The MKT translates 'your love is better than wine' (singular), but the Hebrew is technically plural — the many expressions or instances of love. The comparison to wine (yayin) sets the standard of covenant celebration; wine gladdens the heart (Ps 104:15) and this beloved's love surpasses even that.
3: שֶׁמֶן (shemen) — anointing oil, also the word for richness/fertility. The connection between name (shem) and oil (shemen) in this verse is a deliberate paronomasia (wordplay): the name is like oil poured out — the same consonants (sh-m) in both words make the sound play audible in Hebrew. שְׁמֶךָ שֶׁמֶן מוּרָק (shimkha shemen muraq) = 'your name is like purified oil' — name and character are inseparable in Hebrew anthropology. The young women (alamot) who love him respond to his character-fragrance, not just his appearance.
4: מָשְׁכֵנִי (moshkeni) — 'draw me' uses the verb mashakh, which also describes the drawing of a sword (Ps 28:3) and the attraction of nets; it implies active pulling rather than mere beckoning. The sudden shift from 'let him' (third person, v2-3) to 'you' (second person direct address) is characteristic of the Song's fluid pronoun use — the beloved is sometimes addressed directly, sometimes spoken about. חֲדָרָיו (chadarav) — 'his chambers' — the inner room of the royal household; the most intimate space. The shift to first-person plural 'we will rejoice' includes the daughters of Jerusalem as witnesses.
5: שְׁחוֹרָה אֲנִי וְנָאוָה (shechorah ani venava'ah) — 'I am dark and lovely.' The conjunction is ve- (and), not but — the darkness and the loveliness are not in tension but simultaneous. Some translators import a contrast ('dark but lovely') that the Hebrew does not require. The similes — tents of Kedar (a nomadic Arab tribe whose black goat-hair tents were well known) and curtains of Solomon — pair the wild nomadic beauty with royal elegance. The darkness is the result of outdoor labor, not a deficiency; the beloved defends herself against social judgment.
6: כַּרְמִי שֶׁלִּי (karmi shelli) — 'my own vineyard' — the first-person emphatic reflexive. Throughout the Song the vineyard is a double image: literal (agricultural labor) and metaphorical (the body, sexuality). The brothers' anger (nibeharu — from bahar, to be hot, to flare) caused her to tend their vineyards at the expense of her own; she was compelled to outdoor labor while her personal vineyard was unattended. The 'vineyard I have not kept' sets up the thematic tension between external obligation and personal flourishing.
7: שֶׁאָהֲבָה נַפְשִׁי (she'ahavah nafshi) — 'the one my soul loves' — a phrase repeated five times in the Song (1:7; 3:1, 2, 3, 4). Nefesh here functions as the intensified self, the whole person's longing — not soul in the Greek dualistic sense but the totality of the person expressing desire. נוֹהֶה (noheh) — 'resting, grazing at midday' — the midday rest of shepherds in the shade was a practical necessity in the Near Eastern climate; the beloved asks not for romance but for practical information about where to find him.
8: הַיָּפָה בַּנָּשִׁים (hayafah bannashim) — 'the most beautiful of women' — the superlative in Hebrew is expressed with the article and comparison (literally 'the beautiful one among women'). The response addresses the woman as the most beautiful, not dismissing her practical question but answering it with an elevated address. צְאִי (tze'i) — 'go forth' — feminine imperative. The instruction to follow the tracks of the flock and pasture her own kids (gediyotayikh, feminine plural diminutive) mirrors the beloved's tending her brothers' vineyards — she who was forced to neglect her own now has her own flock to tend.
9: סוּסָתִי (susati) — specifically the feminine form, 'my mare.' The comparison is striking and culturally loaded: in Pharaoh's cavalry, mares were not used in battle — the Egyptians used stallions. An enemy's introduction of a mare into the battle horses could create chaos. The beloved's beauty is compared to a single mare among Pharaoh's stallions — the one who would captivate and throw the entire military formation into disruption. The comparison celebrates her power and magnetic attraction, not her domestication.
10: תֹּרִים (torim) — 'strings of beads' or 'pendants'; the root tor may relate to turtle-doves (similarly named in Hebrew) or to the geometric arrangement of jewelry. חֲרוּזִים (charuzim) — 'strings of beads' — from the root meaning to bore or pierce; the strung beads frame the face and neck. The jewelry description is not merely decorative; in the ANE, specific jewelry types indicated social status, marital status, and tribal affiliation. The beloved's jewelry adorns and places her.
11: נַעֲשֶׂה (na'aseh) — 'we will make' — the plural subject is the companions, attendants, or the chorus. The promise of gold ornaments studded with silver (nequdot hakesef — silver dots/studs) represents the social community's endorsement of the beloved; she is not simply desired by the beloved but acknowledged by the community around him. The plural 'we' includes the audience in the gift-giving.
12: בְּשַׁלְחָנוֹ (beshulchano) — 'at his table' (literally 'while at his table circling/reclining'). Meshivah — a variant reading for the dining posture. נֵרְדִּי (nerdi) — 'my nard' — the first-person possessive on the spikenard (the expensive imported aromatic) claims it as her own fragrance-identity. The nard perfume in response to the king's presence at table creates a sensory exchange: his presence draws out her fragrance.
13: צְרוֹר הַמֹּר (tzeror hamor) — 'a sachet of myrrh' — the term tzeror (bundle, pouch) is used for a bag of money (Gen 42:35; Prov 7:20) and a bundle of tied things. The myrrh (mor) was the most expensive imported resin from Arabia and Africa, used in anointing oil (Exod 30:23) and in burial spices. Its placement 'between my breasts' is the location of the myrrh sachet used medicinally and aromatically — and also the location of the beloved's heart. The beloved is between the body and the anointing of the sacred.
14: כֹּפֶר (kofer) — 'henna blossoms' — a hapax in this noun form, derived from the word for atonement/ransom (kaphar). The henna plant (Lawsonia inermis), used throughout the ancient Near East for dyeing and fragrance, grew abundantly at En-gedi. The wordplay between henna (kofer) and atonement (kippur) is likely intentional in a wisdom book: the beloved who is like a henna cluster from the place of fresh water by the Dead Sea carries a resonance of both beauty and sacred covering.
15: עֵינַיִךְ יוֹנִים (einayikh yonim) — 'your eyes are doves' — the dove (yonah) appears repeatedly in the Song (1:15; 2:12, 14; 4:1; 5:2, 12; 6:9). The dove's associations: gentleness, monogamy (dove pairs are faithful), the Spirit of God (Gen 8:8-12; Matt 3:16), and the sacrifice of the poor (Lev 5:7). The dove-eyes suggest purity, devotion, and gentle perceptiveness — not merely the color or shape but the character expressed in the eyes.
16: עַרְשֵׂנוּ (arsenu) — 'our bed' — the dual possession, signaled by the first-person plural suffix. The bed (eres) is not the marital bed exclusively but the resting place under the open sky — the bower of green (ra'anan) suggests a natural canopy of living branches. The house described in v17 with cedar beams and cypress rafters may be metaphorical — the forest itself as a house.
17: קוֹרוֹת בָּתֵּינוּ (korot bateinu) — 'the beams of our house' — cedar (erez) and cypress (berosh) were the most prestigious building materials in the ancient Near East; Solomon's temple was built of these (1 Kgs 6:15-16). The description of a house made of forest materials — or describing the forest itself as a house — suggests that the beloved and her companion inhabit both the palace and the natural world, or that the grandeur of their love surpasses even the royal building materials. The plural 'our' again asserts shared possession.
